data.title: Trading In My Military Uniform For The Markets: My Transition From The Navy To Seeking Alpha
data.description:
Introduction

When I joined the U.S. Navy at 18-years-old, two months out of high school, I honestly didn't know what I was getting myself into at the time.

Before joining, I planned to do the same as many Americans. Go to college, work a traditional job, retire in my 60s, and ride off into the sunset.

After a family member suggested I try the military, I took and passed the ASVAB test, signed on the dotted line, and the rest is history. My career flowed by, and after about 10 years, I realized I was in it for the long haul.

After visiting various countries , several duty stations, seven U.S. warships, multiple deployments, and 21 years later, I decided it was time to retire.

In this article, I discuss my transition from the military to Seeking Alpha, what got me into investing, and my advice to new contributors on how to succeed on the platform.

How My Military Service Shaped Me for the Transition

During my time in the military, I learned a lot of things about life. However, finance wasn't one of them. My career in the Navy as a Boatswain's Mate was the farthest thing from finance.

But lessons from visiting multiple countries, experiencing different cultures, and seeing my fellow veterans struggle post-retirement is what initially piqued my interest in finance.

So, I started reading investment magazines. And anything related to Warren Buffett.

After completing my first two deployments and saving a bit of money, I was stationed in Texas.

This is where I first dipped my toes into investing. I gained knowledge from my chief, as he often mentioned taking a simple approach by investing in no-load, no-fee mutual funds.

He also mentioned buying and holding blue-chip stocks like The Coca-Cola Company (KO), PepsiCo (PEP), and Procter & Gamble (PG).

Although I would listen and often invest, I didn't have the mental capacity to actually become a successful investor.

It wasn't until a few years before retirement that I actually took investing very seriously. The next chapter of my life was rapidly approaching, and I wanted to avoid what many face after the military - financial struggles.

Approaching Retirement Made Me Take Investing Serious

After getting stationed in California at 18 years in, I figured it was time to take investing more seriously. It was there I started researching and soaking in as much knowledge as I could.

Taking all the lessons from Warren Buffett and my chief about buying and holding quality investments for the long term, this is where dividends came in.

Although most view dividend investing as boring and tax inefficient since dividends are double taxed, I saw it the opposite way. Who wouldn't want to collect passive income for simply buying and holding an investment?

Stumbling Upon Seeking Alpha

Becoming very interested in dividends, I often researched dividend stocks. I'm still not quite sure how I stumbled upon Seeking Alpha, but it was most likely from a Google search.

Shortly after discovering the site in 2021, I was hooked. I would spend countless hours after work and on the weekends reading articles.

I didn't want to end up like most of my fellow veterans. Having to go right back to work after retirement from a lack of planning.

I wanted dividends to supplement my income in retirement to alleviate the pressure of having to work full time. After being on Seeking Alpha, I was introduced to REITs (XLRE) and business development companies (BIZD).

This is how I found authors like Brad Thomas and Hoya Capital. Fast forward a few years later, and I never thought I would be asked to work with them in an Investing Group.

Brad and I co-authored quite a few articles before his transition away from the platform. I've even had the chance to co-author a few REIT-focused articles with Cash Flow Venue as well.

REITs and BDCs are distant cousins that were both created by Congress. By law they're required to pay out 90% of their taxable income in the form of dividends. Because of this, they typically have higher yields.

The higher the yield, the more income you can collect now. But like many investors, I'll admit I got caught up in chasing high yield.

After getting burned by Medical Properties Trust (MPT), highly touted here on Seeking Alpha a few years ago, I decided it was time to make a change to my investment strategy.

Instead of focusing primarily on yield, I shifted to quality. While quality stocks typically have lower yields, these usually have strong cash flows and can continue rewarding shareholders with dividend increases.

Buying quality usually leads to capital appreciation instead of capital loss, like lower-quality, higher-yielding investments. This also is what provoked me to become a Seeking Alpha analyst as well.

With retirement approaching and entertaining job offers, I decided to give it a try in May 2023, five months before my actual retirement from the U.S. Navy. After working with the editors, I finally published my first article titled The Dividend Collectuh's 18 Stock Tim Duncan Portfolio.

My goal on Seeking Alpha was simple: To help others. I wanted to do this by taking a simple approach to investing in dividend stocks and always giving my honest opinion.

I usually only analyze stocks and ETFs that I consider high quality. Companies with strong cash flows, growing earnings, and staying power.

Almost three years, close to 800 articles, and approximately 8,500 followers later, my success on Seeking Alpha has turned me from a military retiree to a full-time analyst.

My Success on Seeking Alpha

My transition from the military to Seeking Alpha has been quite fun. I honestly didn't expect to be here nearly three years later. Or that I would be working in an Investing Group with people that I looked up to on the platform.

I remember being new here and reaching out to a few successful analysts whose articles I enjoyed reading; one who I actually talked to was Julian Lin. I reached out for advice on how to be successful on Seeking Alpha.

One of the things I remember he said was to be honest and consistent. This was easy for me, as I had spent 21 years serving in the Navy. Everything was about honor, courage, and commitment. And also consistency.

My Advice to Other Contributors

If I were to give advice to new contributors on Seeking Alpha, or anyone looking to become one, I would say always be honest with your analysis. And to be consistent. These two things are what I believe helped me grow the following I have so far.

My main goal since starting on Seeking Alpha has remained the same. Help others. I've always said if my articles help at least one person make a better investment decision, then I'm happy.

Another thing I suggest is to be open to learning from others. I can't tell you how much I've learned from readers in the comment section. I'm always being introduced to new stocks and funds or gaining a new perspective on the overall market.

That's why I feel it's so important to engage with your readers, even when comments are critical of your analysis. You can always learn something. Taking this approach, it's highly likely you will not only grow your knowledge but also a decent following as well.

Conclusion

Being a reader here on Seeking Alpha has expanded my investment knowledge. Becoming an analyst, working with editors, and engaging with readers and other analysts has only helped me.

Joining the military at such a young age gave me an advantage. This allowed me to successfully retire and transition to Seeking Alpha as a full-time analyst.

And after nearly three years, I've enjoyed my tenure while vastly expanding my investment knowledge. Writing on this platform has shaped my (investment) strategy throughout the years. If you're a new analyst or someone looking to become one, don't be afraid to start.

data.title: Amazon's DNA: Why Hoarding Cash Is Secondary To Building Empires
data.description:
Amazon (AMZN) actually ended a successful year with its Q4 2025 figures. However, the market reacted negatively, particularly in view of the CapEx forecast for FY 2026, triggering a sell-off. Following this reaction, the share price is now 17% below its all-time high.

Q4 2025 was great

Revenue rose by 13.6% in Q4 2025, slightly faster than in Q3 2025, when growth was 13.4%. This is the third consecutive quarter with faster growth momentum. In the past three years, Amazon has only managed to grow revenue faster in Q4 2023, when growth was 13.9%.

The cloud business in particular saw a significant increase in revenue to 23.6%. In recent years, Amazon's cloud only grew faster in Q3 2022, at 27.5%. AWS growth in particular has been crucial for the share price performance for many years, and in my view, Amazon delivered in full in Q4 2025. In absolute terms, Amazon generated $2.5 billion in additional revenue QoQ from Q3 2025 to Q4 2025. In the same period last year, i.e., from Q3 2024 to Q4 2025, the newly generated revenue was only $1.33 billion. Overall, Amazon generated almost twice as much new revenue from AWS in a year. Andrew Jassy rightly highlighted this growth momentum in the earnings call:

As a reminder, it's very different having 24% year-over-year growth on a $142 billion annualized run rate than to have a higher percentage growth on a meaningfully smaller base, which is the case with our competitors.

The bottom line was also impressive. The gross margin rose from 47.3% to 48.5% YoY, reflecting a less sharp rise in the cost of revenues, which rose by 11.2%. The operating margin rose YoY from 11.3% to 11.8%, resulting in an 18% YoY increase in operating income from $21.2 billion to $24.97 billion.

The elephant in the room

Amazon has announced CapEx of $200 billion for FY 2026, up 50% from FY 2025.

We expect to invest about $200 billion in capital expenditures across Amazon, but predominantly in AWS because we have very high demand, customers really want AWS for core and AI workloads, and we're monetizing capacity as fast as we can install it.

At first glance, this is quite a bombshell, as CapEx of $200 billion is significantly higher than the operating cash flow of $140 billion in FY 2025.

The $60 billion gap sounds large at first glance and will dry out Amazon's free cash flow, which already fell 71% YoY to $11 billion from $38 billion in Q4 2024. So, investors have to swallow that FCF is likely to be negative in FY 2026. Conversely, a temporary gap of $60 billion is hardly significant for a company with a $2.6 trillion market cap. AWS alone generated operating income of approximately $46 billion in FY 2025. Amazon as a whole is therefore investing four times the operating income of its most valuable and fastest-growing business segment. Amazon also has $86 billion in cash and cash equivalents.

Accordingly, I consider it very unlikely that Amazon will overstretch itself with the CapEx announced for FY 2026.

Amazon has always had to invest heavily in its infrastructure. Two decades ago, these were logistic centers for Amazon's retail business, which, by the way, grew 10% in Q4 2025. Based on those investments, Amazon became the e-commerce king.

Then, Amazon had to invest in its data centers for Amazon's cloud business, AWS. Here again, Amazon built an empire and became the market leader.

So overall, Amazon always had to invest first to build a moat that would later enable it to generate billions in revenue and cash flows.

Furthermore, Amazon is acting from a position of strength today. Its operating business has never been as profitable as it is now, with an operating margin of 11.8%.

However, considering the operating margin of 11,8% of the total business, AWS's operating margin of 35% is more than three times that. So if I want to invest in a business area as a company, I will naturally choose the segment where I can not only grow the fastest, but also be the most profitable.

Amazon is known for not placing much emphasis on profits. Jeff Bezos once characterized Amazon as "we are famously unprofitable." It is in Amazon's DNA to invest cash flow in growth rather than profit.

And in view of the competition and investments made by Microsoft (MSFT) and Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG), Amazon has no choice but to keep up in order to avoid losing market share.

I, too, see a realistic opportunity for Amazon to build an AI empire alongside its existing retail and cloud empires, combining AI with its classic AWS services.

While AWS could be at a slight disadvantage compared to Alphabet, as it does not own a traditional LLM, the latest earnings call made it clear how well Amazon is building AWS into a scalable, reliable infrastructure for AI applications. And maybe, the fact that AWS is AI-agnostic could even be an advantage, as customers in AWS Bedrock decide for themselves which LLM to use:

In fact, most sophisticated AI applications leverage multiple models, whether customers want frontier models like Anthropic's Claude or open models like Mistral or Llama, Frontier Intelligence with lower cost and latency like Amazon Nova or video and audio models like TwelveLabs or Nova Sonic. Amazon Bedrock makes it easy to use these models to run inference securely, scalably and performantly. Bedrock is now a multibillion-dollar annualized run rate business and customer spend grew 60% quarter-over-quarter.

Even though Amazon relies on third-party models for the LLM layer, the underlying AWS infrastructure (partly) runs on Amazon's very own Trainium chips. This means Amazon saves on the "Nvidia tax" and can therefore offer AWS to customers at a low price without diluting its own margin.

We've landed over 1.4 million Trainium2 chips, our fastest ramping chip launch ever. Trainium2 is 30% to 40% more price performance than comparable GPUs and is a multibillion-dollar annualized revenue run rate business with 100,000-plus companies using it as Trainium is the majority underpinning of Bedrock usage today. We recently launched Trainium2, which is up to 40% more price performance than Trainium2. We're seeing very strong demand for Trainium3 and expect nearly all of our Trainium3 supply of chips to be committed by mid-2026.

In addition, Amazon sees the trend toward an agentic workforce and has already integrated corresponding capacities into AWS. AWS is thus expanding with more capabilities, including security features, and is increasingly becoming a one-stop suite for enterprises.

Looking ahead, the primary way companies will get value from AI is with agents, some their own, some from others, and there are several customer challenges that we're well positioned to solve. It's harder to build agents than it should be. For that, we've built Strands, a service enabling agents to be created from any model. Once agents are built, enterprises are apprehensive about deploying to production because these agents need to securely and scalably connect to compute, data, tools, memory, identity, policy governance, performance monitoring and other elements. This is a new and hard problem where a solution has not existed until we launched Bedrock AgentCore. Customers are quite excited about AgentCore, and it's unlocking deployments.

In my view, what Amazon is doing here is in line with Jeff Bezos' "Day 1" mentality. Given the strong demand Amazon sees from customers for cloud services, investments in FY 2026 should generate high cash flows in the future (caveat below), which should drive the share price back up.

Since Amazon traditionally reinvests every spare cent, I don't really look at the PE ratio for Amazon (even though it is pretty low compared to the 5y median).

Since high CapEx will also weigh on FCF, I consider operating cash flow to be a key metric for determining Amazon's upside potential. Looking at the P/CFO, we see that Amazon is trading at the lower end of the range seen in recent years. And that's despite the fact that growth is accelerating noticeably, especially in the highly profitable cloud business.

I see no reason why Amazon should not be trading above 20x CFO. In this respect, multiple expansion alone justifies a double-digit upside for the share price. I see further upside potential in the range of 15% to 20% per year due to the growing operating business.

Risk to my thesis

First of all, the high CapEx will have a significant impact on FCF in the short term and will likely result in negative cash flows in FY 2026. In addition, CapEx will have a moderate negative impact on earnings in the short to medium term due to depreciation. From a fundamental perspective, there is therefore a risk that investors will consider sharply declining FCF and the negative impact of CapEx on profit development to justify a lower P/CFO multiple.

However, I consider a multiple of 16.3x to be very low, and I think it is likely that the market's attention will shift back to medium- to long-term growth potential rather than short-term CapEx impact.

From an operational perspective, there is also a risk that current expectations for AI will not be met and that the high (partly circular) investments will not be offset by corresponding efficiency gains at the enterprise level. This would reduce the monetization opportunities of the compute created by hyperscalers such as Amazon. Investors should factor this risk into their calculations. However, given the strong growth in demand for AI features, I believe it would be unreasonable not to respond with the corresponding investments. Because if Amazon doesn't do it, Alphabet will.

Conclusion

Measured by operating cash flow, Amazon shares are currently trading at a discount to their long-term average, even though the underlying business is booming, seeing accelerating growth momentum, and expanding faster than it has in years. I consider the high CapEx to be justified because it contributes directly to the growth of the highly profitable and rapidly growing cloud business. I therefore see the current decline in the share price as a good buying opportunity. Therefore, Amazon is a buy at the current level.

data.title: NuScale Power: Death Spiral Risk (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
NuScale Power (SMR) is the stock I regret the most. I still had hopes that I was wrong when I covered it last year, but I should have shorted. At the time, my position was pragmatic; I decided to have some confidence in the management and wait till the year 2025 ended. To be fair, the hope was built on sharp movement up in case they signed 2-3 clients as the market was waiting for evidence, and so did I.

But today, I would like to revisit my last thesis and do some reality checks. The year has ended, and the main catalyst—contracts—did not appear. The market has punished the company since then, almost 50% down. Now I believe this ~$16 per share price is becoming dangerous. I have seen in other forums that investors were left as bagholders in the company that is actively burning cash, and the horizon for catalysts seems to be empty. I believe it is not a risky growth story anymore, but given current market conditions, I hate to call anything like that, but it is dead money.

What I Was Expecting

Let's revisit what I was expecting last time. Last time I was writing that the management was expecting firm orders till the end of 2025. This was a KPI that I was hoping they would reach. Without real commercial contracts, NuScale technology, even though innovative, in financial terms remains just an expensive science project.

I believe that the market was pricing in this hope too. I remember that one of the readers summarized bull sentiment perfectly: “The share price will be above $60 if they manage to get at least one real contract by the year's end.” At the time, $36 per share was already pricing in the perfect scenario. To be fair, it might have been the reason why I was rating it a hold rather than a sell.

Sadly, 2025 ended without any breaking news, which I was hoping for. It's important to understand when trading story stocks that in capital markets, when a company publicly promises to reach a goal and gives the date, silence is not neutral, and it ends up in a sell-off, speaking from my experience. The silence when the public is awaiting results is considered bad news. This pause that we are seeing is signaling that either conditions are not satisfying the clients or the clients are cautious about new technology for commercial use. As I said, the market hates uncertainty, and at this time, the silence was louder than any words.

Dilution Maths

This might be the most important argument why I am changing this stock’s rating to strong sell. The technology is still the same, projects are still the same, but the financial structure, I believe, is becoming a trap. In August 2025, when I wrote my second thesis, the hold rating was based on the company's solid cash reserve, which should have lasted them for a long time. Let's not forget the quite high share price (~$36), which also allowed me to expect that any capital attraction would be minimally painful to investors. Sadly, the math changed.

Look at the numbers. Let's say that for NuScale to continue its operations and project development, they need $300 million (this is quite realistic for this type of company).

  • Situation in August 2025 (share price $36), to get $300 million, the company needed around 8.3 million new shares. That would have been a painful but understandable 6-7% shareholder dilution.
  • Now, at the current price ($16), to attract the same $300 million, the company would be forced to issue almost 18 million new shares.

Given that they like this type of financing, it means that the capital became twice as expensive. Every dollar that NuScale is spending is costing twice the price it was 6 months ago. In my opinion, it creates a classical death spiral. This happens when the share prices are dropping and the company is forced to issue more shares to reach the same result.

Q3 Did Not Give Hope Either

If in August I was speculating about potential, in November the company announced Q3 results, which were, let's call them, bad. The market was expecting that the loss per share (EPS) would be around -$0.13; sadly, it was -$1.85. So why am I saying it's bad? Because it was a negative surprise of 1300%. The revenue is stagnating. Even though it did rise to $8.2 million, it still does not meet analyst expectations ($11.5 million). It's important to understand that all the revenue comes from engineering services for current projects and not from sales. This proved my main fear that NuScale is more consultancy than industrial nuclear reactor manufacturer.

Strategic Partnership

One of the reasons I believe it is a strong sell is that the main shareholder, Fluor (FLR), decided to sell part of their shares. They sold at the peak, earning around $600 million, and officially announced their plans to sell the remaining shares until mid-2026. So, we are going to see pressure on the stock price either way; if it's going to go up due to contracts, FLR will just be selling at higher prices, which would become pressure. Traders call it resistance.

When the partner, which knows technology and commercial preparedness, is trying to get out, this becomes a red flag. This is a signal, that they do not believe in this investment or they know something we don't.

Catalysts

Currently, I believe the only hope is the RoPower project in Romania, but in a shareholder meeting that should be held on February 12-13 about the final FID. But I believe it is just another bureaucratic step. I believe that even in the best scenario, the first reactor will start operating only in 2027-2028, but this is my opinion. Given this, I also expect 2 years of constant cash burn without any profitability.

At this time, NuScale is using the ATM program; in Q3, they issued 13.2 million new shares to attract the capital. In December, they issued approval to increase authorized shares from 332 million to 662 million, and I think that is final proof that dilution is not only being continued, but it is going to continue in the future. While I did not talk about many catalysts here, it's simply because I can't; there are no potential catalysts that I see.

Summary

My hold rating in August was a mistake. I tried to be more conservative and trusted that the management would be able to convert the experiment to commercial contracts. Today, I see only bad results: EPS is going down, the main partner is leaving, and dilution is almost guaranteed to happen again. At the current price, I still believe there is room to go down, and I will be changing my rating to strong sell. I believe that NuScale became an example of how an innovative company can become a financial void for investors.
